coredata.dc:title: Size and diluted magnetic properties of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots: Monte Carlo study
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica A: Statistical Mechanics and its Applications
coredata.dc:creator: Masrour, R.
Jabar, A.
coredata.dc:description: The magnetic properties of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots have been investigated by varying their sizes with the Monte Carlo simulation. The magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities have been studied with dilutions x (magnetic atom), several sizes L (carbon atom) and exchange interaction J between the magnetic atoms. The all magnetic susceptibilities have been situated at the transitions temperatures of each parameters. The obtained values increase when increases the values of x , L and J . The effect of exchanges interactions and crystal field on the magnetization has been discussed. The magnetic hysteresis cycles for several dilutions x , sizes L , exchange interactions J and temperatures T . The magnetic coercive increases with increasing the exchange interactions and decreases when the temperatures values increasing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Monte Carlo simulations
Graphene
Quantum dots
Magnetic properties

The magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities with dilution x = 0 . 1 , 0.2, 0.3 for J = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , h = 0 . 2
T and L = 48 .

The magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities with sizes L = 16 , 30 and 48 for J = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , h = 0 . 2
T and x = 0 . 3 .

The magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities with exchanges interactions J = 0 . 2 , 0.7 and 1 K for L = 48 , Δ = 0 . 0 , h = 0 . 2
T and x = 0 . 3 .

The magnetizations versus the exchange interactions J for x = 0 . 1 , 0.2, 0.3, L = 48 (a) and L = 16 , 30, 48, x = 0 . 3 (b) with T = 1 . 0 , Δ = 0 . 0 and h = 0 . 2
T .

The magnetizations versus crystal field for x = 0 . 1 , 0.2, 0.3, L = 48 (a) and L = 16 , 30, 48, x = 0 . 3 (b) with T = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 and h = 0 . 2
T .

head.ce:title: Size and diluted magnetic properties of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots: Monte Carlo study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Materials, Processes, Environment and Quality, Cady Ayyed University, National School of Applied Sciences, 63 46000, Safi, Morocco

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Materials, Processes, Environment and Quality, Cady Ayyed University, National School of Applied Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The magnetic properties of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots have been investigated by varying their sizes with the Monte Carlo simulation. The magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities have been studied with dilutions x (magnetic atom), several sizes L (carbon atom) and exchange interaction J between the magnetic atoms. The all magnetic susceptibilities have been situated at the transitions temperatures of each parameters. The obtained values increase when increases the values of x , L and J . The effect of exchanges interactions and crystal field on the magnetization has been discussed. The magnetic hysteresis cycles for several dilutions x , sizes L , exchange interactions J and temperatures T . The magnetic coercive increases with increasing the exchange interactions and decreases when the temperatures values increasing.

ce:section.ce:para:
In recent years, magnetic, graphene, single layer of graphite with excellent conductivity has stimulated the interest of scientists due to the possibilities of several applications in the field of nano-electronics [1,2], graphene based polymer nanocomposites [3–5] and graphene quantum dots [6]. In other hand, the graphene–diamond hybrids have recently attracted researchers to theoretical predictions of the interface between the two allotropes and their electronic properties, using ab initio density functional calculations [7–9], effective field theory [10] and experimentally [11]. Graphene under a uniform magnetic field described by the two-dimensional Dirac oscillator model is discussed based on the approach of the effective mass and the zeta function. Thermodynamic properties of graphene, such as the free energy, the mean energy, the entropy and the specific heat have been found [12]. Vacancy defects in single-layer graphene and silicene exhibit unusual interesting electronic and magnetic properties [13] emerging from their honeycomb structures and rotation symmetries. Recently, we have demonstrated [14] the magnetism of silicene under mono-vacancy, divacancy and substituted atoms such as Al and P. In other hand the energetic stability, electronic, and magnetic properties of hydrogenated graphene nanoflakes using density-functional theory are also studied [15]. We note that in addition of the effect of crystal structural, the magnetic anisotropy can also depend on the size of the nano-crystals [16]. The effects of edge disorder and roughness for graphene nanoribbons in relation to their armchair or zigzag orientation is also studied [17]. Magnetic properties of the main allotropic modifications of carbon (diamond, graphite, nanographite, nanotubes, fullerenes) are described [18] and the magnetic properties of nanoparticle, nanoisland, nanoribbon, metallic nanotube and nanowire are studied with Ising model [19–24]. In a magnetic field, these edge states, strongly localized near zigzag edges, generate a rational fraction of the magnetic flux in each hexagon, and thus behave like zero-field edge states [25]. Furthermore, defective zigzag graphene nanoribbons structures show magnetic moment by supercell up to 2.0
μ
B
[26]. The Ni nanotubes and nanowires with an average diameter of about 140 nm are synthesized by a template-assisted sol–gel autocombustion method using nickel nitrate, citric acid and ammonia as the starting materials [27]. The effect of transition metal atoms on the magnetic properties of nano, bilayer and trilayers-graphene, have been systematically studied by effective-field theory and first principles calculations [28–31]. The behavior of ground state phase diagrams of a mixed Ising spins model on a bipartite square lattice of spins-2 and 5/2 have been also investigated [32]. This the mixed spin-2 and spin-5/2 Ising ferrimagnetic system is used on a layered honeycomb lattice in which Fe
II
( S = 5 ∕ 2 ) and Fe
III
( σ = 2 ) occupy sites [33]. In this paper, the diamond shaped graphene quantum dots Ising model with the single-ion anisotropy is suggested in Fig. 1. The system is formed with diamond shaped graphene quantum dots constituted with carbon atoms diluted by magnetic ion with spin S = 5 ∕ 2 such as Sm
3 +
, Dy
3 +
, Mn
2 +
, Fe3+ etc. In the Section 2 we define the model and theory and in Section 3 the used method. Resulting magnetic properties are presented and discussed in Section 4. The final Section 5 is devoted to conclusions.

In previous works, the critical properties of the randomly site-diluted two, three and four-dimensional Ising model were studied using Monte Carlo simulations [34,35], Wang–Landau algorithm [36] and mean field theory [37].

The diamond shaped graphene quantum dots can be described by Ising model with the crystal field and the Hamiltonian is given as follows:
(1)
H = − J
∑
i , j
η
i
η
j
S
i
S
j
− Δ
∑
i
η
i
S
i
2
− h
∑
i
η
i
S
i
η is the probability of the presence of magnetic atom.

with η = 0 for the presence of carbon in site and η = 1 for the presence magnetic atom with spin-5/2; that is, we have as probability distribution P
(
η
i
)
= p δ
(
η
i
− 1 )
+ q δ
(
η
i
)
. Where J > 0 is the interaction constant, ferromagnetic interactions between spins
S
i
fixed at the lattice sites and taking on values of ± 5/2; ± 3/2 and ± 1/2.

The magnetic properties of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots is assumed to reside in the unit cells and the system consists of several size L = 16 , 30, and 48 carbon atoms. The substitution of carbon atom by magnetic atom is used (see Eq. (1). We apply a standard sampling method to simulate the Hamiltonian given by Eq. (1). In other hand the free boundary conditions on the lattice were imposed and were generated by sequentially traversing the lattice and making single-spin flip attempts. The flips are accepted or rejected according to a heat-bath algorithm under the Metropolis approximation. Our data were generated with 105 Monte Carlo steps per spin, discarding the first 104 Monte Carlo simulations. Starting from different initial conditions, we performed the average of each parameter and estimate the MCSs, averaging over many initial conditions. Our program calculates the following parameters, namely:

Internal energy per site E is given by the following equation:
(2)
E =
1
L
H

Magnetization of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots is given by the following equation:
(3)
M
S
=
1
L
∑
i
S
i

Magnetic susceptibility of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots is given by the following equation:
(4)
χ
S
= β
M
S
2
−
M
S
2
where β =
1
k
B
T
, T denotes the absolute temperature and
k
B
is the Boltzmann’s constant.

We have presented in Fig. 2, the magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities with dilution x = 0 . 1 , 0.2, 0.3 for J = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , h = 0 . 2
T and L = 48 . The magnetization at T = 0 K, is equal the saturation magnetization
M
max
= 2 . 5 . The all magnetic susceptibility are situated at the transitions temperatures. The obtained values of transitions temperatures of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots for different dilution x = 0 . 1 , 0.2 and 0.3 are 0.42, 0.8 and 1.4 K, respectively for a fixed size L = 48 . The transition temperatures increase with increasing the dilution x .

Fig. 3, shows the magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities with sizes L = 16 , 30 and 48 for J = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , h = 0 . 2
T and x = 0 . 3 . The transitions temperatures of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots are found 0.42, 0.82 and 1.40 K, respectively for a fixed dilution x = 0 . 3 . The transition temperatures increase when increase the size from L = 16 to 48.

Fig. 4 illustrates the magnetizations and magnetic susceptibilities with exchanges interactions J = 0 . 2 , 0.7 and 1 K for L = 48 , Δ = 0 . 0 , h = 0 . 2
T and x = 0 . 3 . We have also deduced the transitions temperatures of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots for different exchange interactions J = 0 . 2 , 0.7 and 1 K. The obtained values are 0.8, 1 and 1.4, for a fixed size L = 48 and x = 0 . 3 . We see that the transition temperatures increase when increase the exchange interactions.

Fig. 5 (a)–(b) presents the magnetizations versus the exchange interactions J for ( x = 0 . 1 , 0.2, 0.3, L=48) and ( L = 16 , 30, 48, x = 0 . 3 ), respectively with T = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 and h = 0 . 2
T . For J < 0 , the magnetization decreases with increasing the dilution x . For J > 0 , the magnetization increases when the dilution x increases. For ( J = 0 , M = 0 . 57 ), the magnetization is independently of dilution x (see Fig. 5 (a)). For J < 0 , the magnetization decreases with increasing the sizes L . For J > 0 , the magnetization increases when the sizes L increases. For ( J = 0 , M = 0 . 57 ), the magnetization is independently of sizes L (see Fig. 5 b). Hence it can be deduced that ( J = 0 , M = 0 . 57 ) is a critical point, the variation of the magnetization does not depend on size L , nor on degradation x .

The magnetizations versus crystal field for ( x = 0 . 1 , 0.2, 0.3, L = 48 ) and ( L = 16 , 30, 48, x = 0 . 3 ) is shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b), respectively with T = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 and h = 0 . 2
T . The magnetization increases with increasing the crystal fields (see Fig. 6 a and b). For a fixed value of crystal field the magnetization increases when the dilution x increases (see Fig. 6 (a)) and increases with increasing the sizes of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots (see Fig. 6 (b)). In Fig. 6 a and b, the saturation magnetization increases when dilution x and sizes L .

Finally, we have presented in Fig. 7 (a)–(d) the magnetic hysteresis cycles for ( x = 0 . 1 , 0.2, 0.3, T = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , J = 1 . 0
K , L=48), ( L = 16 , 30, 48, T = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , J = 1 . 0
K , x = 0 . 3 ), ( J = 0 . 2 , 1.0, 2.0 K, T = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , L = 48 , x = 0 . 3 ) and ( T = 0 . 2 , 1.0, 2.5 K, J = 1 . 0
K , Δ = 0 . 0 , L = 48 , x = 0 . 3 ), respectively. In Fig. 7 a and b, the system exhibits the superparamagnetism behaviour for a weak values of exchange interactions we see that it is independently of dilution x and sizes of the of diamond shaped graphene quantum dots. The ferromagnetic hysteresis cycle is obtained when the exchange interactions increase (see Fig. 7 (c)) and the magnetic coercive field increases with increasing the exchange interactions. The similar behavior is observed in previous works [38,39]. The ferromagnetic cycle has been shown in Fig. 7 (d). The magnetic coercive field decreases when the exchange interactions increase. These results are similar to those obtained by experiments results given in experimental observation of superparamagnetism in manganese clusters study [40].

In this work, a diamond shaped graphene quantum dots is described as Ising model with spin-5/2. The effects of the exchange coupling, sizes and dilutions x on the magnetization and the susceptibilities of the system have been studied. The transition temperatures are deduced for different exchange interactions, dilution x and sizes. the superparamagnetism behavior is found for a weak values of exchange interactions and the ferromagnetic hysteresis cycle is also established when the exchange increases. The magnetic coercive field increases with increasing the exchange interactions and decreases with increasing the temperatures values. These are in agreement with the experimental results.
